High-quality distractors include q1-d1 (confirmation bias link), q3-d1 (plausible-anchor-only misconception), q5-d2 (prior-belief claim), q6-d2 (read-first-then-adjust), and q6-d3 (awareness-alone misconception), all directly addressed in the corpus.

learning_objectives: LO1 — Define anchoring bias in plain language and identify its two core components: the initial anchor and the adjustment heuristic
LO2 — Explain why the adjustment heuristic consistently produces insufficient adjustment — why people stop adjusting too soon
LO3 — Recall the original Tversky and Kahneman wheel-of-fortune experiment as empirical evidence that anchoring bias is real, measurable, and independent of the relevance of the anchor
LO4 — Identify at least three ways anchoring bias appears in AI-assisted work, including first-pass AI outputs acting as anchors
LO5 — Distinguish anchoring bias from confirmation bias by describing the mechanism that drives each one
LO6 — Apply awareness of anchoring bias to a self-check question: 'What number or starting point did I see first, and am I still too close to it?'

beginner_fairness_and_scope.note: All stems use plain language. System 1 / System 2 are prior-topic vocabulary (9.1, 9.2). Confirmation bias is prior-topic vocabulary (9.3). All distractors represent plausible beginner misconceptions grounded in this topic's corpus. No out-of-scope concepts used as answer options.

verdict: Pass — length-bias fix verified (cycle-1 rate 1.00 reduced to 0.17); all five checks clean.
